facts_disclosed: Transferred ordination to UCC (United Church of Christ) from Episcopal

turns.content: I did something last month that I have been discerning for nearly three years, and now that it is done I feel both lighter and a little lost. I transferred my ordination. I am no longer Episcopal. I have moved to the United Church of Christ.

Three years of discernment is serious, and I can understand why something that brings relief can also bring disorientation. A denominational transfer isn't just an administrative change -- it's a shift in spiritual home. That's significant, especially after what sounds like a long relationship with the Episcopal tradition.

What started the discernment?

It is hard to point to a single moment. The Episcopal church is beautiful -- I want to say that clearly and without qualification. The liturgy, the Book of Common Prayer, the way the language holds you even when your faith does not. I was formed by that tradition. It taught me how to pray, how to read scripture, how to sit in the cadences of a collect and feel held by something larger than myself. I will carry that formation for the rest of my life, and I am grateful for it.

That's a generous and honest way to honor what you're leaving. You're not rejecting it -- you're carrying it with you while moving toward something else. The fact that you can name what the Episcopal tradition gave you without bitterness suggests this really was discernment, not reaction.

So what did the UCC offer that the Episcopal church couldn't?

Questions. That is the simplest answer, though it is not simple at all. The Episcopal church has a deep intellectual tradition, but it operates within a framework of received answers -- the creeds, the catechism, the shape of the liturgical year. Those answers are beautiful and I do not dismiss them. But in my work, I sit with people who are dying, and the questions they ask do not fit inside those frames. They never have, if I am honest. And I found myself, over years, inhabiting a faith that was more question than answer, and the Episcopal structure could not hold that. Not because it is rigid -- it is remarkably generous for a liturgical tradition -- but because the generosity has limits, and I kept pressing against them.

That makes sense. There's a difference between a tradition that tolerates doubt within a framework of certainty and one that treats doubt itself as a valid mode of faith. It sounds like you moved past the point where the Episcopal framework could contain your questions, not because the framework is bad, but because your questions had outgrown it.

How does the UCC hold those questions differently?

The UCC has a saying -- 'God is still speaking.' There is a comma, not a period. And I know that sounds like a bumper sticker, and perhaps it is, but underneath it is a genuine theological commitment to openness. What I found in the UCC is permission to sit with my questions rather than paper over them. You can stand in a UCC pulpit and say 'I do not know what happens after death' and that is not a failure of faith -- it is a form of faith. It is the faith of someone who has sat at enough bedsides to know that mystery is not a problem to be solved but a reality to be inhabited.

The comma rather than the period -- that's a powerful distinction, bumper sticker or not. It implies that revelation is ongoing, that the tradition doesn't have the final word. And for someone in your work, where the received answers keep running into lived experience that doesn't conform to them, that kind of openness isn't just intellectually appealing. It's spiritually necessary.

Was the transfer itself difficult? Logistically or emotionally?

Emotionally, yes. Logistically, less so -- the UCC is remarkably welcoming of clergy from other traditions, which is itself telling. But emotionally, there was a Sunday about a year into the discernment when I attended the Eucharist at my Episcopal parish and I realized I was performing something I no longer fully inhabited. The words were still beautiful. The communion was still sacred. But I was a guest in my own house, and I wonder if you know that feeling -- when the thing that held you for decades quietly stops holding you, not because it failed, but because you changed.

That image -- a guest in your own house -- is devastating and precise. You didn't leave because the house was bad. You left because you woke up one day and realized you'd been slowly moving toward the door for years, and the house hadn't noticed because the liturgy kept going whether you were fully present or not.

That's one of the dangers of beautiful ritual, isn't it? It can sustain you when your faith falters, which is a gift. But it can also mask the moment when you've genuinely moved on, because the beauty keeps you in the pew even after you've left in every other sense.

That is exactly it. The beauty kept me longer than I should have stayed. And I do not regret that time -- it was its own kind of discernment, the slow realization that what I love about the Episcopal church is not the same as what I need from a spiritual community. I love the aesthetics, the language, the depth of the tradition. But I needed a community that asks different questions. Questions without predetermined answers. The UCC gave me that, and it gave me colleagues who are also chaplains and social workers and people who sit with suffering and come back to church on Sunday with the same holy uncertainty I carry.

The distinction between what you love and what you need is crucial, and not many people are honest enough to separate those. Loving something doesn't obligate you to stay in it, especially when staying would require you to be less than fully yourself.

And finding colleagues who share that holy uncertainty -- that matters enormously. Faith practiced in isolation is one thing. Faith practiced in a community of people who also sit with suffering and bring their questions to the table without apology -- that's sustaining in a way that solitary doubt can't be.

It is. I went to a UCC clergy retreat last month and a woman stood up and read a poem by Mary Oliver instead of a scripture passage, and no one flinched. And then a man talked about his doubt as if it were a companion rather than an enemy, and a woman who runs a homeless shelter said she had not prayed in three months but she had held a dying woman's hand and wondered if that counted. And I thought: yes. This is where I belong. Not because it is comfortable -- it is disorienting in its own way -- but because it is honest.

That scene you're describing -- the Oliver poem, the doubt as companion, the hand-holding as prayer -- that's a community that has earned its openness. Those aren't people who are casual about faith. They're people who take it so seriously that they refuse to pretend. That's a very different thing from not caring.

I'm glad you found it. Three years is a long time to sit with a question that large, and the fact that you let it take that long tells me this wasn't impulsive or reactive. It was the kind of slow, careful leaving that honors both what you're moving from and what you're moving toward.

Thank you for that. I think I needed to tell someone who would not immediately ask me whether the UCC is 'liberal enough' or 'too liberal' or whatever the political question is. It is not a political decision. It is a theological one, and more than that, it is a vocational one. My work asks me to hold space for people who are asking the most fundamental questions a human being can ask, and I needed a community that would hold space for me to do the same. The comma, not the period. I will live inside that comma for the rest of my life, I suspect, and I have made my peace with that.

Living inside the comma -- that's a beautiful way to carry this forward. And you're right that reducing it to a political choice misses the point entirely. This is about what kind of spiritual container can hold the weight of the questions your work generates. The Episcopal church held you beautifully for a long time. The UCC holds you now, differently but truly.

Thank you for sharing this. Three years of discernment, a lifetime of formation, and the courage to change -- that's not a small thing. I think the comma suits you.
